answer:
**In chapter 27 of *Diwan-E-Zauq*, the opening couplet lists four physical features of the beloved: the downy line (*khat*, referring to the fine down or mustache line on the cheek), curls (*kaakul*, locks on the forehead), tresses (*zulfen*, long flowing locks of hair), and locks (*gesu*, disheveled hair).**[^1]

These are poetically described as "advancing" or growing in the court of Beauty, portrayed as the "darkest of her foes" due to their black color contrasting with her fairness.[^2]

No other physical features of the beloved are explicitly listed in the remaining couplets, which shift to themes of reconciliation, the lover's growing hair and nails, rivals, sorrow, and tears.[^3]

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 27 ("Khat barha kaakul barhe zulfen barhein gesu barhe"), [p-d4f83b](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/27-khat-barha-kaakul-barhe-zulfen-barhein-gesu-barhe/translation/#p-d4f83b).

[^2]: Ibid., [transliteration p-38b039](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/27-khat-barha-kaakul-barhe-zulfen-barhein-gesu-barhe/transliteration/#p-38b039) and [original p-7b54a0](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/27-khat-barha-kaakul-barhe-zulfen-barhein-gesu-barhe/original/#p-7b54a0).

[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 27, [full chapter (translation)](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/27-khat-barha-kaakul-barhe-zulfen-barhein-gesu-barhe/translation/).
